Most movements are comprised of sequences of movements that are learned through practice.
From the complex routines that gymnasts perform to intricate dance numbers to simply reaching for an object, our movements are made up of sequences.
The cerebellum is an area of the brain that is involved in learning and executing these sequences.
Patients with cerebellar abnormalities often have difficulty executing sequences of movement sequences, and are unable to learn sequences at all.
However, it is not clear how this happens.
Previous studies have focused on single-component movements, in which a single movement consists of only one or two components.
These studies have shown that the cerebella can learn and execute sequences of single component movements, but not sequences of multiple components.
This suggests that it is possible for the brain to learn sequence-based movements by using feedback signals from other parts of the body.
To test this idea, Choi et al.
used a technique called eyelid conditioning, which involves stimulating the brain’s eyelid muscles with mossy fibers.
This causes the eyelids to move in a specific direction in response to an external stimulus.
The brain then uses this feedback signal to learn the next movement component of the sequence.

Imagine a gymnastics competition in which participants take turns to cartwheel and somersault across the floor.
The routines on display comprise sequences of precisely timed movements learned through practice.
This is also true for many of the actions we perform every day, such as reaching for a cup of coffee.
A region of the brain called the cerebellum helps us learn sequences of movements.
But how does it do this?
To find out, Khilkevich et al.
came up with a new version of an old experiment.
Rabbits were first trained to blink their eye in response to a specific external cue.
This type of learning, called associative learning, has been shown before in the cerebellum.
But Khilkevich et al.
wondered whether the cerebellum could also use internal feedback signals from the eyeblink as a cue to learn the next movement?
If so, this might explain how the cerebellum can chain movements together in a sequence.
As predicted, Khilkevich et al.
found that rabbits could learn to blink their eye in response to an initial signal, and then blink again in response to the first blink.
Control experiments confirmed that the second eyeblink was coupled to the first, and not to the original cue.

Mammalian cells use a protein called mTOR to control the growth of their tissues and organs.
This protein is made up of two proteins, mTOR and mTORC2, which are part of a larger protein complex.
The mTOR complex consists of mTOR, which acts as a central regulator of cell growth and metabolism, and a second protein complex, which is responsible for regulating the activity of other proteins.
When mTOR is not working properly, cells can develop diseases such as diabetes, cancer and Alzheimer’s disease.
The protein mTOR interacts with other proteins to control these processes.
However, it is not clear how mTOR binds to these other proteins, or how it is able to bind to rapamycin, a drug that blocks mTOR.
Now, Saxton et al.
have used a technique called cryo-electron microscopy to study the structure of the mTOR-mTOR complex in human cells.
This revealed that mTOR forms a complex with two other proteins: Raptor and SIN1, which together form a subunit called RaptorC2.
This suggests that Raptor-1 is the core protein of the complex, whereas SIN-2 is the subunit that binds to mTOR
ref:
To grow and multiply, a living cell must take a variety of factors into account, such as its own energy levels and the availability of nutrients.
A protein called mTOR sits at the core of a signaling pathway that integrates these and other sources information.
Problems with the mTOR pathway contribute to several diseases including diabetes and cancer.
The mTOR protein occurs in two distinct protein complexes, called mTORC1 and mTORC2.
These complexes contain a mix of other proteins – known as accessory proteins.
They also sense different cues and act upon distinct targets in the cell.
Recent research reported the structure of mTORC1, which provided clues about how this complex works.
Yet, much less was known about the mTORC2 complex.
Stuttfeld, Aylett et al.
have now used a technique called cryo-electron microscopy to reveal the three-dimensional architecture of the human version of mTORC2.
Comparing the new mTORC2 structure to the existing one for mTORC1 showed that they have many features in common but important differences too.
The overall shape of both complexes is similar and each complex contains two copies of mTOR arranged in a similar way.

Our perception of number and space are tightly intertwined.
In humans, small numbers are mapped to the left and large numbers to the right.
This spatial arrangement of numbers is evident in preverbal infants, non-human primates, and even birds.
It has been suggested that this co-localization is the result of a process called ‘cortical recycling’, in which circuits that process space take over those that process numbers.
However, it is not clear how this process works.
Does numerical processing replace space processing, or does it augment it?
To find out, Dehaene et al.
studied the effect of visual motion direction on the brain’s ability to process numbers and space.
The experiments show that a region of the brain called the lateral inferior parietal cortex ( or LIP for short ) adapts to the direction of movement.
This process is similar to that seen in the visual cortex of the left side of the eye, and is thought to be similar to the process that occurs in the parietal lobe of the right hemisphere of the head, which is responsible for spatial processing.
In particular, LIP and VIP both contain neurons that are sensitive to small numbers, but the LIP area is less sensitive to large numbers.
ref:
Our sense of number is thought to have emerged from the circuits of cortical neurons in the brain that originally represent space, a process known as 'cortical recycling'.
Accordingly, our perception of space and number are tightly intertwined: for example, people think about numbers on a mental number line, where smaller numbers are mapped to the left, and larger numbers are mapped to the right.
Also, damage to a brain region called the parietal cortex disrupts both space and number processing.
If number processing recycles the neurons that encode space, which form does this appropriation take?
Recycling could preserve the original behavior of the neurons ( processing space ), thus enriching the neurons’ functional repertoire with a new capacity ( processing number ).
Alternatively, the newly developed role could replace the original one, such that space and number cohabitate the same brain area but use separate neurons.
To disentangle these hypotheses, Schwiedrzik et al.
used a technique called 'perceptual adaptation'.
Here, continuously showing someone a particular feature eventually exhausts the neurons that respond to that feature.
Neurons that respond to the opposite feature are however less exhausted and dominate perception.
Consequently, people perceive the opposite of what they are adapted to.

Stem cells are a type of stem cell that can develop into a wide variety of specialized cells, including neurons, blood cells, and epithelial cells that line the surface of organs such as the brain and the skin.
However, it is not clear how these specialized cells respond to the nutrients they receive from their environment and how this affects their ability to differentiate into specialized cells.
One protein called Musashi-1 is essential for the development of stem cells, but it is unclear how this protein regulates the differentiation of these cells.
To investigate this question, Sakakibara et al.
used a technique called small molecule screening to look for molecules that interfere with the activity of a protein that controls stem cell differentiation.
The experiments show that a molecule called oleic acid inhibits the activity ( or binding ) of this protein.
This protein binds to a region of RNA called the RNA Recognition Motif ( RRM ) and prevents the RNA from being translated into proteins.
This prevents the stem cells from dividing into the different types of cells that they need to develop into.
In the brain, the RRM protein is found in stem cells called astrocytes and glial cells, which are the progenitor cells of the nervous system.

When an embryo is developing, stem cells must divide and develop into many specialized types of cells.
However, if cell division doesn't stop, or if it restarts later in life, it can cause tumors to form.
Musashi-1 is a protein that binds to molecules of RNA and helps to promote cell growth during development: mice that lack this protein have serious brain defects and die shortly after birth.
Musashi-1 is usually turned off in adult cells that are not dividing.
Sometimes, however, it remains active and contributes to the growth of cancers in the brain and the gut.
Reducing Musashi-1 levels in colon tumors slows their growth and causes the cancer cells to die.
To find a compound that would switch off Musashi-1, Clingman et al.
screened more than 30, 000 compounds and identified four inhibitors.
One of these was oleic acid, a fatty acid that is found in olive oil and other animal and plant oils.
Oleic acid interacts with Musashi-1 in a way that changes the shape of the protein.
These changes mean that Musashi-1 is no longer able to regulate the genes that control cell proliferation.

Nerve cells communicate with each other by sending electrical signals across long-lasting connections known as synapses.
These connections are formed by chemical molecules called neurotransmitters, which are released from the cell’s outer membrane via a process called synaptic vesicle release.
These vesicles can be replenished by the release of calcium ions into the cell via a series of chemical reactions.
The release of the calcium ions is controlled by a group of proteins called the synaptotagmin family.
These proteins bind to calcium ions and release them from the outer membrane of the cell, which is then reabsorbed by the cell.
However, it is not clear what role these proteins play in the synapse, or how they interact with other proteins that are present in the cell membrane.
Syt 7 is a protein that is found in almost all animals, yet it is unclear what role it plays in neurons.
Previous studies have suggested that syt 7 acts as a ‘sensor’ for releasing calcium ions, but it was not clear how this works.
Now, Wang et al.
have used a combination of genetic and biochemical approaches to investigate the role of syt7 in neurons in the central nervous system of mice and zebrafish.
ref:
Neurons communicate with one another at junctions called synapses.
The arrival of an electrical signal called an action potential at the first neuron triggers the release of chemicals called neurotransmitters into the synapse.
These chemicals then diffuse across the gap between the neurons and bind to receptors on the second cell.
The neurotransmitter molecules are stored in the first cell in packages known as vesicles, which release their contents by fusing with the cell membrane.
Following a fusion event, neurons must replenish their vesicle stocks to ensure that they are ready for the arrival of the next action potential.
This replenishment process is known to involve a calcium-dependent pathway and a calcium-independent pathway.
A protein called calmodulin, that binds calcium ions, has an important role in the first of these pathways.
Now, Liu et al.
have shown that another protein, synaptotagmin 7, also has a key role in the replenishment of synaptic vesicles, possibly as a sensor for calcium ions.
Moreover, Liu et al.
found that synaptotagmin 7 and calmodulin bind to each other to form a complex, which suggests that the calcium-dependent replenishment pathway is regulated by this complex.

Mood and anxiety disorders are the most common psychiatric conditions and are exacerbated by stress.
Treatments for these disorders are currently ineffective because they are only partially effective.
Drugs that block the activity of a protein called cyclooxygenase-2, or COX-2 for short, have been shown to reduce anxiety- and depression-related behaviors in mice.
However, it is not clear whether these drugs are also effective in humans.
To investigate this question, Hermanson et al.
studied mice that had been exposed to a variety of stress-induced behaviors, including anxiety-like behavior, memory loss, and fear, in a mirror chamber test.
The experiments show that both COX2 inhibitors and a new type of drug called SSCI, which blocks its activity, reduce the severity of these behaviors in the mice.
In addition, SSCIs also reduce the number of nerve cells in the amygdala, the brain region that controls anxiety and depression.
The researchers found that the COX inhibitors reduced anxiety-related behavior in mice that were exposed to stress, and that the SSCAs also reduced the amount of fear in these mice.
These findings suggest that the effects of these drugs may also be mediated by the activation of cannabinoid receptors on the brain
ref:
People may experience a stressful or traumatic event that can cause anxiety in their day-to-day lives.
For some people, this anxiety can persist and develop into a disorder, such as posttraumatic stress disorder ( PTSD ) or acute stress disorder.
Millions of people worldwide suffer from these disorders, but there are few classes of drug treatments currently available.
Some preliminary studies in mice have suggested that anti-inflammatory drugs called cyclooxygenase-2 ( COX-2 ) inhibitors may relieve symptoms of anxiety.
COX-2 inhibitors target an enzyme called cyclooxygenase-2, which is produced in many tissues throughout the body and brain.
Some COX-2 inhibitors, such as Celebrex, are already widely used to treat arthritis.
Studies in people have also suggested these drugs given in combination with antidepressants might also help people with depression, while a recently developed COX-2 inhibitor, called LM-4131, stops the breakdown of chemicals produced in the brain that interact with the same brain receptors that marijuana does.
In animal studies, this new drug reduced anxious behaviors.
However, scientists didn’t know if the drug would also reduce anxious behaviors after animals were exposed to a traumatic or stressful experience.

Membrane proteins are the building blocks of membranes that connect cells to each other and to the outside world.
These proteins are essential for many biological processes, such as cell signaling and energy metabolism.
However, membrane proteins are difficult to study because they are insoluble in water and are often difficult to work with in aqueous solutions.
To study membrane proteins, researchers often use a method called ‘detergent micelles’, which are made of a mixture of water and a detergent called detergents.
The proteins are then separated from the detergent mixture and reassembled into small particles that can be used to study the structure and activity of the membrane proteins.
This method is expensive and time-consuming, but it has been shown to be effective for studying membrane proteins in the laboratory.
The problem is that membrane proteins need to be reconstituted into water-soluble particles, which can be difficult to do in a liquid solution.
To overcome this problem, researchers have developed a new method called the peptidisc, which uses a short molecule called a bi-helical peptide to form a scaffold around a membrane protein.
This scaffold then wraps around the membrane protein and forms a protective shell around the protein.
ref:
Surrounding every living cell is a biological membrane that is largely impermeable to water-soluble molecules.
This hydrophobic ( or “water-hating” ) barrier preserves the contents of the cell and also regulates how the cell interacts with its environment.
This latter function is critical and relies on a class of proteins that are embedded within the membrane and are also hydrophobic.
The hydrophobic nature of membrane proteins is however inconvenient for biochemical studies which usually take place in water-based solutions.
Therefore, membrane proteins are under-represented in biological research compared to the water-soluble ones, even though roughly one quarter of a cell’s proteins are membrane proteins.
Researchers have developed a few tricks to keep membrane proteins soluble after they have been extracted from the membrane.
An old but popular technique makes use of detergents, which are chemicals with opposing hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties ( hydrophilic literally means “water-loving” ).
However, even mild detergents can damage membrane proteins and will sometimes lead to experimental artifacts.
More recent tricks to stabilize membrane proteins without detergents have been described but remain laborious, costly or difficult to perform.

Transcription is the process by which DNA is copied into messenger RNA molecules ( or mRNAs ), which are then translated into proteins.
Errors in this process can lead to mutations in the DNA, which can cause diseases such as cancer and Alzheimer’s disease.
However, it is not clear how many errors there are, or how they affect the function of proteins.
To find out, researchers need to be able to identify errors in the entire genome of a cell.
One way to do this is to use a technique called ‘rolling-circle sequencing’, which uses a method called amplification to amplify the DNA sequences of individual genes.
This method has been used in eukaryotes such as bacteria and yeast, but it has not yet been applied to other organisms such as fungi and protozoa.
Now, Gout et al.
have developed a rolling-circle-sequencing method that can be applied to the genomes of bacteria, yeast and other prokaryotic species.
The method was used to identify thousands of errors in bacterial DNA sequences, and it was able to do so accurately and efficiently at a large scale in all three species of bacteria.

Most cells contain molecules of DNA that carry instructions to make the proteins cells need to perform different tasks.
When a cell requires a certain protein, the corresponding DNA sequence is first transcribed into molecules of ribonucleic acid ( RNA ) known as transcripts.
These sequences of RNA are then read by the cell and translated into the desired protein sequence.
Errors in copying DNA before a cell divides, can lead to genetic mutations that affect the ability of the cell to carry out certain roles, influencing the overall ‘fitness’ of the cell.
Similar to genetic mutations, errors that arise when forming RNA transcripts may also alter the tasks a cell performs.
However, it is difficult to find out what kinds of errors cells have in their transcripts and how often these mistakes occur.
This is because current methods for sequencing RNA are prone to technical inaccuracies that interfere with the ability to detect true transcript errors.
Now, Li and Lynch have adapted a method for high-throughput sequencing of RNA, which can accurately identify transcript errors in Escherichia coli and other species of bacteria.
The experiments showed that errors in RNA molecules occurred more frequently than genetic mutations in the same sequence of DNA.

After losing a hand, simple tasks such as tying your shoelaces can become a daily challenge.
How do different individuals adapt to such challenges, and what role does brain plasticity play in this adaptation?
By studying the brains of individuals with congenital or acquired hand absence, we hope to shed light on the scope for adaptive plasticity in the adult human brain, thereby providing important information for informing future neurorehabilitation strategies.
Previous studies have focused on how the brain adapts to the loss of a hand or arm.
However, these studies have largely focused on sensory deprivation and maladaptive changes that occur when the brain is deprived of the sense of touch, such as when the face or arm is moved away from the body.
This suggests that the brain may not be able to adapt to these changes as quickly as previously thought.
To address this issue, Jang et al.
have now used a combination of brain imaging and computer modelling to investigate how brain activity changes in individuals with and without a hand following amputation.
The experiments involved 20 healthy volunteers who had either an intact hand or a hand that had been amputated below the elbow.
The volunteers were asked to move their left arm, right arm, or left hand around the body using a computer
ref:
The loss of a limb will have a profound impact on an individual’s daily life.
Nevertheless, individuals can employ a variety of behavioural strategies to adapt to the loss of, say, a hand.
Some become skilled at using the residual part of their arm, while others prefer to rely on their other hand.
Their brain, too, will undergo major changes.
Many studies have shown that the region of the brain that controlled a given limb can be “taken over” by another part of the body if that limb is lost.
This process has been previously considered to be harmful, as it has been linked to experiences of pain arising from the missing limb.
Now, Makin et al.
have explored the links between changes in the behaviour of individuals missing a hand and changes in their brains.
People who had been born without a hand or who had lost a hand in later life were asked to wear a device that recorded their movements as they went about their daily lives.
The data revealed that people who had been born without a hand made relatively more use of their residual limb, while those who had lost their hand made relatively more use of their remaining hand.
Moreover, these differences were reflected in patterns of brain activity.

Tetrapods are a group of animals that evolved from small land-dwelling animals that lived in the water and later moved to the land.
These animals had long bones, which supported their weight and allowed them to move around.
The long bones of tetrapods, such as the batrachids and amniotes, are among the most studied skeletal elements in the field of anatomy because they are crucial for the development and biomechanical properties of the appendicular skeleton, which is made up of bones and cartilage.
The bone marrow, which contains the cells needed to make red blood cells, is found in the bone marrow of mammals and many other animals.
However, in fish, the blood cells are produced in the fins of the fish, which are found at the tip of the tail, and in the kidneys and liver of the ray-finned fish.
When did long bones start producing blood cells?
Recent studies have suggested that long bones may have been the first animals to produce blood cells.
This suggests that the long bones might have protected the blood-producing cells from the harsh environment of the land and protected them from damage caused by exposure to UV radiation.
To investigate this hypothesis, Kapp et al.
studied the bones
ref:
For many aquatic creatures, the red blood cells that rush through their bodies are created in organs such as the liver or the kidney.
In most land vertebrates however, blood-cell production occurs in the bone marrow.
There, the process is shielded from the ultraviolet light or starker temperature changes experienced out of the water.
It is possible that this difference evolved long before the first animal with a backbone crawled out of the aquatic environment and faced new, harsher conditions: yet very little fossil evidence exists to support this idea.
A definitive answer demands a close examination of fossils from the water-to-land transition including lobe-finned fish and early limbed vertebrates.
To support the production of red blood cells, their fin and limb bones would have needed an internal cavity that can house a specific niche that opens onto a complex network of blood vessels.
To investigate this question, Estefa et al.
harnessed the powerful x-ray beam produced by the European Synchrotron Radiation Facility and imaged the fin and limb bones from fossil lobe-finned fish and early limbed vertebrates.
The resulting three-dimensional structures revealed spongy long bones with closed internal cavities where the bone marrow cells were probably entrapped.

Alcohol use disorder is a major public health problem that affects millions of people around the world.
People with alcohol use disorder often struggle to cope with everyday stressors, such as anxiety and depression, and are more likely to drink more than others.
This is because the brain is more sensitive to stress than other parts of the body.
For example, the brain’s stress response system is activated by the release of a chemical called Pdynorphin, which is produced in the brain by a group of neurons called the stria terminalis ( or BNST for short ).
However, when these neurons are exposed to the smell of a predator, they become more active and more sensitive than normal.
This suggests that alcohol exposure can affect how the BnST responds to the odor of the predator.
To test this idea, Lê et al.
exposed mice to a compound called trimethylthiazoline ( TMT ) that is found in fox feces.
The mice were exposed to this compound for several weeks, and then had to learn to avoid it.
When the mice were deprived of alcohol for a period of time, they showed abnormal responses to the TMT-induced stress response.

The connection between stress and alcohol use is highly complex.
On one hand, there is the idea of having a drink to ‘steady the nerves’.
On the other hand, in alcoholics, abnormal responses to stress often accompany heavy drinking.
In this case, it remains unknown whether stress cause excessive drinking, or vice versa.
Areas of the brain that normally help respond to stress work differently in long-term, heavy drinkers.
One example is a structure called the bed nucleus of the stria terminalis ( BNST ), which is over-active in anxiety disorders and is also associated with some of the symptoms of alcohol withdrawal.
The mechanism behind both problems is thought to be a specific ‘signaling system’ that is activated by a small molecule called dynorphin.
Previous research into the effects of dynorphin was performed either in the context of alcoholism or of anxiety disorders, but it was not known if there was a connection between the two.
Therefore, Hwa et al.
wanted to determine how prolonged alcohol use might affect responses to stress, and whether dynorphin signaling plays a role.

Cells respond to stress by activating genes that encode molecular chaperones and other proteins called heat shock proteins ( HSPs ).
When exposed to high temperatures, these proteins help the cells to adapt to the changes in their environment and respond quickly and robustly to changes in temperature.
The HSP70 protein is the best-studied example of a heat shock protein, but it is not the only one that is activated by the heat shock response.
In fact, the majority of HSP proteins are inhibited by a protein called HSF1, which is a transcription factor that controls the activity of other genes.
However, HSP 70 is one of the most inducible examples of a HSP protein, and it is highly selective for activation in response to heat stress.
In response to stress, mammalian cells activate a protein known as eEF1A1.
This protein is found in many different types of cells, including neurons and muscle cells, and is known to play a key role in the production of many different proteins.
It is also known to help regulate the translation of mRNA, the messenger RNA molecules that are used to make proteins.

Living cells must be able to withstand changes in the environment.
For example, if there is a sudden increase in temperature—which could damage proteins or other molecules—most cells can respond with the ‘heat shock response’.
In humans and other mammals, a single protein called heat shock factor 1 triggers the production of numerous heat shock proteins that protect the cell from the detrimental effects of high temperatures.
Most heat shock proteins protect cells by binding to, and stabilizing, other molecules in the cell; this prevents these molecules from being damaged or from aggregating and allows them to continue to function as normal.
A protein called eEF1A1 is involved in the final stages of protein production and also enhances the function of heat shock factor 1 during the heat shock response.
To make a protein, an enzyme called RNA polymerase transcribes DNA in the nucleus of the cell into a messenger RNA molecule that then exits the nucleus and binds to a ribosome.
This molecular machine then translates the messenger RNA sequence into a protein by joining together individual building blocks called amino acids in the correct order.
Like other elongation factors, eEF1A1 helps to select the amino acids that match the sequence of the messenger RNA template.

Flowers are important for plant reproductive success and for human sustenance.
The stem cells that make up the plant’s stem cells are called primordia, and they are responsible for producing the seeds that give rise to the flowers.
However, the stem cells themselves do not produce the seeds.
Instead, they are recruited to the flower by a hormone called auxin, which is produced by a protein called MONOPTEROS.
When the hormone is present, it triggers a cascade of events that lead to the cell becoming a primordium, which then produces the seeds, and the rest of the plant.
Plants need to be able to switch between stem cells and primordial stem cells in order to make sure that they produce the right number of seeds.
To do this, the cell must first switch from being a stem cell descendent to being a primordial stem cell, and then switch to becoming the stem cell of the flower.
This switch is triggered by the hormone auxin and is controlled by a group of proteins called Aux/IAA proteins, which bind to and activate a transcription factor called MP.

Plants form new structures such as flowers or branches throughout their life as they develop and grow.
However, most plant cells are not able to produce a new flower or branch because the genes involved in these processes are usually switched off.
The genes are found in regions of chromatin—the structure in which DNA is packaged in plant cells—that are normally tightly packed.
This packing prevents other proteins called transcription factors from accessing the DNA and switching the genes on.
New flowers form from cells that contain high levels of a plant hormone called auxin.
In these cells, a protein called MONOPTEROS switches on genes involved in making flowers.
How the structure of the chromatin that surrounds these genes is altered so that they can be switched on is not clear.
Wu, Yamaguchi, Xiao et al.
studied this question in a plant known as Arabidopsis.
The experiments show that MONOPTEROS plays a crucial role in altering the structure of chromatin to allow flowers to form.
In the presence of high levels of auxin, MONOPTEROS recruits groups of proteins called SWI/SNF remodeling complexes to regions of chromatin that contain genes involved in flower formation.

Myelodysplastic syndromes are a group of genetic disorders that affect the ability of the body’s immune system to recognize and destroy cancer cells.
Patients with these disorders often have abnormal bone marrow and blood cells that are not able to divide properly, which can lead to the formation of blood cancers such as acute myeloid leukemia ( AML ) and multiple myeloma, which is the most common type of blood cancer.
The mutations that cause these disorders are often found on the long arm of the human chromosome 7, which runs from the base of the chromosome to the tip of the tail.
Mutations in this section of chromosome are known as deletions, and many of these deletions are found in patients with a genetic mutation known as chromosome 7 deletion.
However, it is not clear whether deletions affecting this segment of chromosome 7 play a role in the development of leukemia or other blood cancers.
To investigate this question, Elias et al.
have now genetically engineered mice that lack a deletion on chromosome 7.
The mutant mice have a deletion in a section of the 7q22 gene that is most common in people with MDS and AML.
These mutant mice also lack the ability to divide normally, which suggests that the deletion

Stem cells in the bone marrow are essential for creating new blood cells.
Myelodysplastic syndrome ( MDS ) is a common type of blood cancer in the elderly that occurs when blood cells fail to develop normally.
Depending on which types of blood cells are affected, individuals with MDS may bleed more easily, feel weak and tired, or be unable to effectively fight off infections.
Animals and plants store their genetic information in the form of chromosomes.
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes, with one copy inherited from the mother, and the other from the father.
The bone marrow cells of many people with MDS delete a section from one of their copies of chromosome 7.
As this section contains many different genes, it is difficult to fully understand which specific genes contribute to the development of MDS when one copy is lost.
Wong et al.
have now genetically engineered mice to lack a section of one of their copies of chromosome 7 that is often missing in patients with MDS.
Bone marrow cells from these mice exhibit many of the same abnormalities found in human MDS.
For example, most of the immature blood stem cells expand, but these stem cells do not correctly specialize into mature blood cells—in particular, not enough immune cells are produced.

The nervous system is the body’s sensory system, which is responsible for detecting and responding to touch, smell, sound, temperature, and other sensory stimuli.
The brain and spinal cord contain many different types of sensory neurons, each of which has distinct roles in the nervous system.
For example, the dorsal root ganglion ( DRG ) sensory neurons are responsible for the sense of touch, and the pruriceptive and proprioceptive neurons for sense of movement and pain.
The DRG neurons are also responsible for sensing heat, cold, chemical and mechanical stimuli, as well as for sensing pain.
However, it is not clear which types of neurons are involved in each of these sensory processes, or how they are related to each other.
To address this question, Abraira et al.
used a technique called transcriptional profiling to identify the genes that are expressed by the DRG sensory neurons in mice.
This technique uses fluorescent tags on the surface of the cells to identify which genes are switched on and off by specific proteins, and then isolates these genes from the cells.
The technique was then used to determine which genes were switched on or off by the individual cells, and which proteins were switched off or on at the same time.
ref:
In the nervous system, a network of specialized neurons—known as the somatosensory system—carries information about sensations including touch, muscle position, temperature and pain.
Distinct sets of somatosensory neurons are thought to carry information about the different types of sensations.
In young animals, the precise switching on, or ‘expression’, of genes controls the formation of the network of neurons.
However, it is not known exactly which genes are expressed in what types of neurons, where, or when.
Here, Chiu et al.
used a technique called flow cytometry using different fluorescent markers to isolate a group of cells called Dorsal Root Ganglion ( DRG ) neurons in mice.
These neurons have long thread-like fibers that extend from the spinal cord to the skin, muscles and joints all over the body.
These fibers carry sensory information to the spinal cord, where it can be relayed to the brain and processed.
The experiments compared three distinct types of DRG neuron and found that they differed in their ability to send information to other cells.
Chiu et al.
analyzed the expression of all the genes in the three types of DRG neurons.
Each type of neuron had distinct groups of genes that were being expressed.

Cells are made up of two sets of chromosomes, each with two copies of the same DNA.
Each pair of chromosomes has a unique set of letters and numbers, and the two sets are separated by a structure called a chiasma.
This structure is made of a series of proteins called the synaptonemal complex, which assembles between the chromosomes to form a structure known as the ‘chiasma’, and it is thought that this structure helps to ensure that each pair of homologous chromosomes is correctly separated.
However, it is not clear how this process works, or how it is regulated.
The nematode worm Caenorhabditis elegans is a good model organism for studying how chromosomes are separated in the cell.
In this worm, the chromosomes of the two pairs of chromosomes are attached to each other by a complex of proteins, which forms a ‘crossover’ structure between the two chromosomes.
When a cell divides, the DNA on one side of each chromosome is copied and the other is copied on the other side of the chromosome.

Most human cells contain 23 pairs of chromosomes, giving 46 chromosomes in total.
When a cell divides, it typically copies all its chromosomes and distributes the copies equally between the two new cells, so that they also have 46 chromosomes.
Cells in the reproductive organs undergo a special division process called meiosis, which halves the number of chromosomes.
As a result, sperm and eggs have just 23 chromosomes, and so when they combine, the fertilized egg receives a complete set of 46 chromosomes.
A similar process happens in all species that use sexual reproduction.
As the chromosomes prepare to separate, they line up side by side in matching pairs.
During this period, DNA from one chromosome will swap with DNA from its partner.
These events are called “crossovers, ” and because such exchanges can happen at many locations along the chromosomes, no two sperm or eggs are the same.
Historic studies in fruit flies revealed that chromosomes do not mix their DNA at random.
After one crossover occurs, it is less likely that another will happen, and if there are two crossovers, the second one tends be far away from the first.
This suggests that there must be a signal that tells the chromosomes about the exchange.

Cells divide into two new cells at the end of each cycle of life.
This process, known as mitosis, involves a series of events that must be carefully coordinated to ensure that the new cells contain the correct number of chromosomes and the right number of genes.
Mitotic exit is one of the most important events in the cell cycle, as it involves the destruction of a large number of proteins and the dephosphorylation of a number of other proteins.
These events occur in a matter of minutes, and the timing of these events is crucial for the cell’s survival.
One of the major proteins involved in mitosis is a protein called cyclin B, which plays a key role in the separation of the chromosomes.
This protein is destroyed by a group of enzymes called the anaphase-promoting complex ( APC/C ), which also includes two other enzymes called PP1 and PP2A-B55, and two phosphatases, which are thought to be involved in the process of phosphorylation.
However, it is not clear how these enzymes work together to control the timing and order of events during mitotic exit.

The splitting of the two copies of DNA happens through a series of controlled events known as mitotic exit.
Previous research has suggested that mitotic exit relies on both the destruction of specific proteins and the removal of tags called phosphate groups from other proteins.
Phosphate groups modify how proteins behave and their removal can trigger changes in a protein’s activity.
Although protein destruction and phosphate group removal were known to be important to mitotic exit, it was not understood how they are coordinated in the cell to ensure the correct order of events.

Major depressive disorder affects approximately 16% of the world’s population at some point in their lives.
Symptoms of major depression include a lack of motivation, reduced ability to derive pleasure from natural rewards, and abnormalities of sleep and appetite.
It is thought that depression is caused by a combination of genetic, environmental, and biological factors.
The brain region of the brain called the medial prefrontal cortex ( mPFC ) is known to be involved in depression and behavioral resilience.
However, it is not clear what role these cells play in the development of depression and how they respond to stress.
To address this question, Waller et al.
studied mice that had mutations in a gene called Wfs1, which is involved in the production of serotonin, a neurotransmitter that is important for mood and anxiety.
The experiments showed that mice with WFS1 mutations were more likely to experience depressive symptoms, and that they were less able to cope with stressful situations.
Further experiments revealed that the cells in the brain that produce serotonin in response to stress are called superficial layer 2/3 pyramidal cells.
These cells are thought to be important for regulating how the brain responds to stress, but it was not known whether they also play a role in depression.

Multiple genes and environmental factors have been implicated in depression, and one of the strongest risk factors for developing the disorder is exposure to stress.

identified a specific population of cells in the prefrontal cortex that express Wfs1.

Proteins are the building blocks of almost every cell in the body.
They are made up of a chain of amino acids arranged in a three-dimensional ( 3D ) structure.
The structure of a protein is determined by the amino acid sequence of each amino acid in the chain, which determines how it will fold into a specific 3D shape.
However, a protein’s 3D structure is not the only thing that determines how a protein works.
It is also important for the protein to be able to interact with other proteins and other molecules in the cell.
For example, the chaperone protein Hsp90 is involved in the maturation of 20% of the entire proteome, which is a group of proteins called the proteome.
The chaperones help the proteins to work properly by binding to them and changing the way they interact with each other.
This process is called post-translational modification ( or PTM ), and it involves adding small modifications to the protein.

The behavior of proteins depends on their structure; yet, proteins are often flexible and will change shape, much like a tree in the wind.

Therefore, the likelihood that the protein will take on this specific shape directly determines how efficiently that protein can perform a specific job.
The shape of a protein can be regulated by changes at several levels; these could include modifying one of the amino acid building blocks that make up that protein, binding to another protein, or by placing the protein in a part of the cell that is crowded with other large molecules.

The front of a cell is the part of the cell that faces the outside world, while the back is the interior.
The front is made up of a patch of cells called the cortex, which contains a protein called Cdc42, which is activated by a group of enzymes called GTPases.
When a cell divides, it divides into two parts: the front and the back.
This process is called ‘polarization’, and it is thought to be triggered by fluctuations in the concentration of the proteins that make up the cortex.
These fluctuations can be small, but they can also be large enough to trigger the formation of clusters of proteins that form the front of the new cell.
This is called positive feedback.
Positive feedback allows fluctuations in concentration to set off growth of a cluster of polarity factors to establish a front ( called a front-polarizing cluster ).
However, it is not clear how cells can only form one front at a time.

This is possible because newly-formed cells can establish front and back ends that influence how each cell grows and develops.
A protein called Cdc42 – which can cycle between an active and inactive form – establishes this cell “polarity”.

The DNA molecules in cells are continuously bombarded with radiation, chemicals and other agents, and it is important for cells to repair the damage caused by these before the process of cell division begins.
Most DNA molecules consist of two single strands of DNA that are held together by hydrogen bonds in the familiar double-helix structure.
Of the various types of damage that DNA molecules are prone to, double-strand breaks are among the most dangerous because they can lead to cancer if they are not repaired.
DNA molecules use four bases—adenine, cytosine, guanine, and thymine—to store genetic information.
In single-stranded DNA these bases are attached to a backbone made of alternating sugar and phosphate groups.
A crucial feature of double-stranded DNA is that the sequences of bases in the two strands are complementary to each other—adenine is always paired with thymine, and cytosine is always paired with guanine.
However, the hydrogen bonds that hold the pairs of bases together are quite weak, which means that the two strands of the double helix can be pulled apart quite easily.
The ease with which these bonds can be formed and broken is crucial for many genetic processes.

Pneumoviruses are a group of viruses that infect the lungs and cause a wide range of diseases, including pneumonia, whooping cough, and other respiratory infections.
These viruses contain a genome that contains a large number of genes that encode proteins that are required for the virus to replicate.
The virus then uses these proteins to make copies of its genetic material, which is then used as a template to make new copies of the virus’s genetic material.
These copies are then used to make the viral RNA molecules that are needed to replicate the virus in the body.
The viral RNA is packaged into molecules called messenger ribonucleic acids ( mRNAs ), which are then transcribed to make messenger RNA molecules ( mRNA ).
The RNA molecules are then translated into proteins called nucleoproteins, which can then be used as templates to make other proteins.

Lysosomes are organelles in living cells that are responsible for the breakdown and recycling of cellular machinery.
Dysfunctional lysosome systems can lead to diseases such as cystic fibrosis, myotonia, epilepsy and Huntington’s disease, as well as other neurodegenerative disorders such as Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease.
These diseases are caused by mutations in the genes that control the activity of enzymes that break down and recycle the machinery inside the cell.
In these diseases, the enzymes are unable to break down the machinery, which results in the breakdown of the machinery and the loss of its contents.
This in turn leads to a loss of the ability of these enzymes to work properly.
The breakdown of these machinery is carried out by a group of enzymes called enzymes called Lysosomal enzymes.

Lysosomes contain many different proteins including some that break down waste material into recyclable fragments and others that transport the fragments out of the lysosome.

In social interactions, it is important to understand the other person’s thoughts and intentions so that we can form a theory of their mind.
To do this, we need to be able to understand what they are thinking and feel comfortable sharing their thoughts with each other.
One way to do this is to use gaze following, the ability to follow the gaze of another person towards an object of interest.
Gaze following relies on the orientation of the head and the shoulder girdle of the person being followed, which is known as the ‘face patch’.
In humans, this orientation is determined by the position of the eyes and the head.
In monkeys, however, gaze following is largely determined by head or face orientation.

used functional magnetic resonance imaging ( fMRI ) to study the brain regions involved in gaze following in rhesus macaque monkeys.

Gaze following—working out where someone else is looking, and then switching your attention to that position—is an important part of social behavior and learning.
Additionally, it is thought to be an important step towards recognizing that others have a mind of their own.
Humans mostly use eye position to work out the ‘gaze direction’ of someone else, whereas non-human primates rely instead on the orientation of the face.
However, the neural circuits that control gaze following are thought to be similar in both.
Gaze following is a complex process that requires the brain to process a lot of different information.
A face must be recognized, and its orientation worked out.

Finally, the object of interest needs to be recognized and the attention of the observer focused on it.

One eye of a newborn mouse is deprived of its normal patterned vision at birth.
This causes the animal to experience a loss of vision in the other eye, which is referred to as ‘amblyopia’.
If this condition persists for a long period of time, the brain loses the ability to process information from the left eye, and neurons in the visual cortex lose responsiveness through the deprived eye.
This loss of visual acuity can lead to problems with vision in later life, such as blindness and vision loss in the elderly.
However, it is not clear how the brain can recover from this loss.

This suggests that the brain may be able to compensate for the loss of normal vision by increasing the number of brain cells that are activated during movement.
To test this idea, Niell and Stryker used mice that were deprived of their normal vision for 21 days.

Amblyopia, otherwise known as ‘lazy eye’, is a condition in which vision fails to develop normally during childhood, not due to problems with the eye itself but due to problems with the transmission of information from the eye to the brain.
It occurs when disorders such as squint—in which the eyes point in different directions—cause the brain to continually ignore input from one eye, with the result that vision in that eye never fully develops.
If detected in infancy, amblyopia can be treated by surgery, although such interventions must be performed early because they are much less effective when used on adults.

The leaf-cutting ants of the genus Acromyrmex live in a symbiotic relationship with a fungus called Leucocoprinus gongylophorus.
The ants cut leaves from the canopy and understory and bring them back to their nest to be processed as growth substrate for the fungus.
This involves chewing forage material into smaller pieces, which are mixed with fecal fluid and deposited as new substrate on the actively growing parts of the garden.
However, most of the leaves that the ants harvest are heavily protected against herbivory by recalcitrant cell walls and toxic substances ( Chen, 2008 ), which means that the fungus has to be able to break down the leaves before they can be used as a food source for the ants.
The fungus and the ants share many of the same genes, but it is not clear how the fungus and ants evolved to work together in this way.

Colonies of tropical leaf-cutting ants live in underground nests where a fungus grows that feeds them.
The ants, in turn, provide the fungus with the freshly-cut leaf fragments it needs for nutrition.
The relationship between the ants and the fungus, in which they live close together and help one another survive, is known as symbiosis.
It is an ancient, extremely well integrated relationship, in which neither species can survive without the other.
However, the details of how the ants and the fungus work together to break down the leaf fragments so they can be used for nutrition are not well understood.

The ants then make temporary pellets out of the new leaf fragments before providing them to the fungus.

Tuberculosis is a disease caused by a bacterium called Mycobacterium tuberculosis ( or TB for short ).
The disease is caused by the bacterium that causes pneumonia, salmonellosis, and other infections, and is often treated with antibiotics.
However, TB is becoming harder to treat as the antibiotics used to treat it become less effective, and the bacteria that cause the disease are becoming resistant to these drugs.
Antibiotics are commonly used in the treatment of bacterial infections, but they can also interfere with the body’s immune system, which can lead to serious side-effects.

The discovery of antibiotic drugs, which treat diseases caused by bacteria, has been a hugely valuable advance in modern medicine.
They work by targeting specific cellular processes in bacteria, ultimately stopping them from multiplying or killing them outright.
Antibiotics sometimes also affect their human hosts and can cause side-effects, such as gut problems or skin reactions.
Recent evidence suggests that antibiotics also have an impact on the human immune system.
This may happen either indirectly, by affecting ‘friendly’ bacteria normally present in the body, or through direct effects on immune cells.
In turn, this could change the effectiveness of drug treatments.
For example, if an antibiotic weakens immune cells, the body could have difficulty fighting off the existing infection – or become more vulnerable to new ones.
However, even though new drugs are being introduced to combat the worldwide rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, their effects on immunity are still not well understood.

Single-celled organisms, however, must rely on molecular cues to understand their immediate surroundings.

Arabidopsis thaliana is a plant that is used as a model organism to study plant growth, development and reproduction.

These viruses enter cells when two lipid bilayers—one surrounding the virus, the other enclosing the cellular compartment into which the virus has been engulfed—merge to form a single unified membrane.
This process, known as membrane fusion, allows the RNA of the virus to gain access to the host cell's molecular machinery, which it commandeers to produce multiple copies of itself and to direct the assembly of new virus particles.
The process of membrane fusion generally includes an intermediate hemifused state in which only the adjacent monolayers from each bilayer have merged.
In addition to its role in virology, membrane fusion is critical for many other biological processes, including exocytosis, protein trafficking and the fertilization of eggs by sperm.
Efficient membrane fusion requires a catalyst, and a glycoprotein known as the influenza hemagglutinin performs this role for the influenza virus.
The hemagglutinin is found on the surface of the virus, and a typical influenza virus particle can have a few hundred such molecules on its surface.

Cells in the brain communicate with each other by sending electrical signals called neurotransmitters to one another.
These signals travel along the length of a nerve fiber to form synapses with other cells in the same part of the brain, known as the cortex.
The connections between neurons in the cortex are called synapses, and they are thought to be important for many aspects of brain activity, including memory and learning.
However, it is not clear how many synapses are present in each layer of the cortex, and how they work together.
To answer these questions, researchers need to be able to measure the number of connections between different types of neurons in a given cortical layer.
To do this, they need to collect large amounts of data from multiple experiments, and then use these data to build computer models of how the brain works.
This is challenging because the data collected in individual experiments can vary widely between experiments, which makes it difficult to build accurate models that can account for these differences.
To address this problem, Pernice et al.
have now developed a new way to collect and analyze data from a large number of experiments in adult mouse and human cortical layers.

The outer sheet of brain tissue, the neocortex, is composed of circuits formed from trillions of connections among billions of neurons, of which there are about one hundred different neuron types.
The scale and complexity of cortical circuitry pose experimental challenges, leading to an incomplete understanding of how cortical cell types are connected and the computations that take place at the connections.
About half of the cell types in the brain are excitatory, which means they can activate other cells.
The cortex consists of several distinct layers of cells, within which excitatory cells cooperate to process the signals they receive from other cortical layers and brain areas.

measured the likelihood and strength of connectivity among related groups of excitatory cell types in slices of cortex taken from human and mouse brains.

Genes are made up of two sets of chromosomes, each containing two copies of the same DNA.
When a cell divides to produce two new cells, the DNA on one of the chromosomes is copied to form two new chromosomes.
This process, known as meiosis, results in a double-stranded double helix, which is broken at the start of the cell’s first cell division.
The newly formed cells then pass on one copy of each of their chromosomes to the next cell, which in turn passes on the other copy to the new cell.
This is known as intra-chromosomal recombination, and it is thought to be important for the survival of sexually reproducing organisms, such as yeast and humans, because it can introduce new mutations into the DNA of the next generation.

Most living organisms package their DNA into bundles called chromosomes.
These chromosomes generally form pairs, with each chromosome in the pair containing the same number of genes.
The genes also come in the same order, but the exact sequence of DNA bases within the genes can be different.
When sex cells—such as egg, sperm or pollen cells—are made, each pair of chromosomes is separated so that the each sex cell contains only half the normal number of chromosomes.
However, before they are separated, the pairs swap lengths of DNA via recombination events.
These can involve exchanging large chunks of the chromosomes: this is called a ‘crossover’.
Alternatively, short stretches of one chromosome can be replaced by the corresponding region from the other in the pair.
When these ‘non-crossovers’ cause a change in the DNA sequence they are known as gene conversions.
Long-standing questions in the field of plant biology include: how common are gene conversions?
How much DNA is typically exchanged?

Fatigue is a common phenomenon in everyday life, but it is also common in neurological conditions, such as Parkinson’s disease, and is a major cause of disability in people with epilepsy.
It is common practice to train athletes, musicians, surgeons and others to perform strenuous physical activities up to and beyond a point of fatigue.
However, it is not clear how muscle fatigue affects learning new motor skills.
This is partly because it is difficult to separate the effects of fatigue on performance from the effects it has on learning.
For example, while the performance of rats in the absence of a motivational cue seemed to show no learning in a maze task despite repeated practice, providing a food reward uncovered that they indeed had learned the right path nonetheless ( Tolman and Honzik, 1930 ), it was not clear if this was because they were fatigued or because they had not learned the correct way to go.

set out to answer this question by asking healthy volunteers to perform a sequential pinch force task over two days.
Even though the volunteers were not fatigued at the start of the experiment, they were unable to perform the task correctly on the second day.
This suggests that muscle fatigue impairs learning.

Healthy cells can typically repair damage to their DNA with high accuracy, keeping their genetic code intact.
In contrast, cancer cells often lose this ability.

Bacteria are a group of single-celled microorganisms that live in the gut of plants, animals and fungi, and are often used as models for studying how they grow and evolve.
In the laboratory, they are often grown in a culture medium that contains two carbon sources: glucose and malate, which are the two most abundant molecules in the environment.
When both of these sources are available, the bacteria consume them both at the same time, which can lead to rapid growth rates that exceed those achieved with either substrate alone.
However, when the two sources of carbon are consumed simultaneously, the resulting high levels of acetate can be harmful to the cells.
This is because acetate is a weak molecule that can break down into smaller molecules that can be toxic to cells.
The cells that produce acetate are then able to detoxify the toxic molecules and replace them with a non-toxic metabolite called acetoin, which is then used as a carbon source in later growth stages.
This process is called metabolic specialization, and it has been shown that bacteria can specialize to produce different types of carbon.

The chemical reactions that occur within a living organism are collectively referred to as its metabolism.
Many metabolic reactions produce byproducts that will poison the cells if they are not dealt with: fermenting bacteria, for example, release harmful organic acids and alcohols.
How the bacteria respond to these toxins has been most studied at the level of entire microbial populations, meaning the activities of individual cells are effectively “averaged” together.
Yet, even two bacteria with the same genes and living in the same environment can behave in different ways.

The analysis focused on genes involved in fundamental metabolic processes, known as the TCA cycle, which the bacteria use to generate energy and build biomass.
The experiments revealed that, even when all the cells are genetically identical, different Bacillus subtilis cells do indeed specialize into metabolic subpopulations with distinct growth rates.

DNA replication is the process by which a cell duplicates its genetic material to create two copies of itself.
This process is carried out in a process called DNA replication, which involves two strands of DNA being copied from one copy to create a new copy of the same DNA.
The two strands are then joined together to form a double-stranded DNA double helix, which is then used to build a new DNA molecule.
However, this process is often hindered by obstacles in and on the DNA that prevent the two strands from joining together.
For example, the DNA replication machinery can encounter barriers that block the replication of the DNA.
If these barriers are present, the replication machinery cannot complete the duplication process, which can result in the DNA being unable to be duplicated.

Groups of proteins then travel down the DNA strands, separating them as they go, and using them as templates for making copies of the DNA.

Genes are turned into their protein products via two steps.
The first, transcription, produces an intermediate RNA molecule or ‘transcript’; the second step, translation, turns the transcript into a protein.
Most genes in mammals contain stretches of DNA called exons, which code for protein, interspersed with sequences called introns that do not.

Computer models can simplify this task, but only if the models are accurate copies of living systems.

Bacteriophages are a type of virus that invade bacteria; their name literally means ‘bacteria eater’.

The fruit fly, Drosophila melanogaster, is a popular model organism for studying sleep because it is easy to study, and its larvae are easy to manipulate.

A DNA double helix is made up of two DNA strands, which in turn are made of molecules that are each known by a single letter—A, T, C, or G. The sequence of these ‘letters’ in each DNA strand contains biological information.
Genes are sections of DNA that can be ‘expressed’ to produce proteins and RNA molecules.
To express a gene, the DNA strands in the double helix must first be partially separated so that one of them can be used as a template to build an RNA molecule in a process called transcription.
Either of the DNA strands in a helix can be used as an RNA template, but contain different genes and are read in opposite directions.
One of the two strands is called the ‘sense’ strand, the other the ‘antisense’ strand.
The RNA molecule does not transcribe a whole DNA strand; instead, it transcribes a section of DNA, known as a transcription unit, which contains at least one gene.
The end of a transcription unit is marked by certain signals that stop transcription.
However, some transcription units in a DNA strand overlap, so there must be some way that the transcription machinery can sometimes ignore these stop signals.

Putting on a necklace involves holding open a clasp while aligning it with a ring, a process that requires quickly and accurately determining and controlling each object’s orientation.
This task requires the fingertip, which is located at the base of the hand, to detect the orientation of the object.
The fingertip is surrounded by a layer of skin that is sensitive to the movement of objects, and this information is transmitted to the brain via nerve cells called mechanoreceptors, which are located in the glabrous skin of the fingertips.
However, it is not clear how quickly the brain can process this information.
Previous studies have measured the ability of the brain to detect edges of an object, but these studies have focused on edges that span the entire fingertip.
For example, when a fingertip touches an edge that spans the entire surface of a dial, the mechanoreceptor cells in the fingertips activate, and the brain then uses this information to orient the dial towards the target position.
This information is then passed on to the eye, which can then use this orientation information to guide the dial to the correct position.

Putting on a necklace requires using your fingertips to hold open a clasp, which you then insert into a small ring.
For you to do this, your nervous system must first work out which way the clasp and the ring are facing relative to one another.
It then uses that information to coordinate the movements of your fingertips.
If you fasten the necklace behind your head, your nervous system must perform these tasks without information from your eyes.
Instead, it must use the way in which the edges of the clasp and the ring indent the skin on your fingertips to work out their orientation.
Earlier studies have examined this process by asking healthy volunteers to judge the orientation of objects – or more precisely edges – that an experimenter has pressed against their fingertips.
But people perform worse than expected on this task given their manual dexterity.
Pruszynski et al.
wondered whether the task might underestimate the abilities of the volunteers because it involves passively perceiving objects, rather than actively manipulating them.
To test this idea, Pruszynski et al.
designed a new experiment.
Healthy volunteers were asked to use a fingertip to rotate a pointer on a dial to a target location.
The participants could not see the dial, and so they had to use touch alone to determine which way the pointer was facing.

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis ( ALS ) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that affects the brain and spinal cord.
The disease is caused by mutations in the gene that encodes a protein called SOD1, which is involved in the destruction of reactive oxygen species ( ROS ) in the body.
The protein is made up of three subunits, each of which contains two metal ions and a disulfide bond, and is able to bind to and break down ROS molecules.
However, when this protein is misfolded, it can form aggregates that are toxic to cells.
These aggregates can also form in the absence of the metal ions, but it is not clear whether these aggregates cause the disease or if they are caused by the misfolding of the protein itself.
To better understand the causes of ALS, it is important to understand how the protein misfolds and forms aggregates in the first place.
One of the proteins involved in this process is called apoSOD12SH, and it is thought to be responsible for causing ALS in people with familial ALS and sporadic forms of the disease.

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis ( or ALS ) is a disease that affects the nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord, such that more and more of these cells die as the disease progresses.
Since nerve cells direct muscle movement, people with ALS increasingly lose control of their muscles, and may therefore lose the ability to speak, eat, move, and breathe.
Around one in five people who have an inherited form of ALS also have mutations in a gene that encodes an enzyme called SOD1.
This antioxidant enzyme detoxifies harmful chemicals that are the byproducts of normal cellular activity.
The mature active form of this enzyme contains two SOD1 proteins, each of which binds one copper ion and one zinc ion.
Each protein in a pair also contains a strong bond that helps to stabilize its three-dimensional structure.
However, immature copies of this protein, which lack the strong bond and metal ions, often fold into the wrong shape.
These misfolded proteins can clump together into clusters, and potentially lead to the development of ALS.
Efforts to study misfolded proteins are limited by the fact that misfolding is a rare event.

In 1983, researchers discovered potassium channels in rat cardiac myocytes, a type of muscle cell found in the heart.
These potassium channels open when potassium ions are added to the cell’s supply of potassium ions.
When the potassium ions leave the cell, the potassium channels close, but when the potassium is removed, the channels re-open and the potassium currents return to normal levels.
The potassium channels are found in many cells, including the pancreas, muscle cells and nerve cells, and they are thought to play a role in regulating the activity of these cells.

A hormone called insulin finely controls the amount of sugar in the blood.
When the blood sugar content is high, a group of cells in the pancreas release insulin; when it is low, they stop.
In these cells, the level of sugar in the blood modifies the ratio of two molecules: ATP, the body’s energy currency, and ADP, a molecule closely related to ATP.
Changes in the ATP/ADP ratio are therefore a proxy of the variations in blood sugar levels.
In these pancreatic cells, a membrane protein called ATP sensitive potassium channel, KATP channel for short, acts as a switch that turns on and off the production of insulin.
ATP and ADP control that switch, with the two molecules having opposite effects on the channel – ATP deactivates it, ADP activates it.
The changes in ATP/ADP ratio – and by extension in blood sugar levels – are therefore coupled with the release of insulin.
However, how KATP channels sense the changes in the ATP/ADP ratio in these cells is still unclear.
In particular, ATP levels are usually high and constant: ATP is then continuously deactivating the channels, and it is unclear how ADP ever activates them.

Toxoplasma gondii is a parasite that causes toxoplasmosis, a disease that affects approximately one-third of the human population.
This disease is caused by the parasite infecting the blood of humans and other warm-blooded animals, including mice, goats, sheep, and roe deer.
The parasite is a facultative heteroxenous parasite, meaning that it can infect different species of animals in order to survive and reproduce.
However, it is not clear why this is the case, or how it has evolved to be able to survive in such a wide range of host species.
One possibility is that the parasite has evolved a way to evade the immune system of its host, allowing it to persist undetected in the body of an immunocompromised individual.
To investigate this possibility, H. hammondi, a species of parasite that is found in rodents, goats and sheep, was used to study the life cycle of the parasite.
The experiments show that the parasites grow in the laboratory at a rate that is different from that of their host, and that they respond differently to stress.
In particular, the growth of the parasites showed that they were more resistant to stress than their host.

Over a billion people worldwide are infected, often for life, by a parasite known as Toxoplasma gondii.
Most of the time, the parasites remain in a dormant state and cause no symptoms.
However, the parasites can reactivate, which can be fatal for people with weaker immune systems.
Currently no drugs can kill the dormant stages of T. gondii.
One of the possible reasons T. gondii is so widespread is because it easily passes from host to host, and resists both detection and treatment.
Like many parasites, T. gondii infects multiple hosts during its life cycle: it reproduces sexually in its ‘definitive' host, the cat, and reproduces asexually in its ‘intermediate’ hosts, which include virtually all warm-blooded animals.
Unlike most parasites, all hosts can transmit T. gondii to other hosts, making it a very flexible parasite.
However, its closest living relative, Hammondia hammondi, can only be transmitted from a definitive host to an intermediate host, or vice versa.
The reason for this difference remains unclear, but comparisons between these species can hopefully help uncover how T. gondii came to be such a promiscuous parasite.

Genes are made up of strands of DNA, which are packaged into structures called chromosomes.
The DNA is organized into regions called chromosomes, which can be organized into two types: heterochromatin, which is compacted and tightly packed, and euchromatin that is more open and flexible.
Both types of chromatin contain proteins called histones, which help to control the activity of the DNA.
However, there are two major differences between the two types.
The first type is called ‘constitutive’ chromatin, while the second type is known as ‘facultative chromatin’.
In both types, the DNA is tightly packed and the histone proteins that make up the chromatin are switched off.
This allows the DNA to be transcribed to produce messenger RNA ( or messenger RNA for short ) molecules that can be used to make proteins that are needed for the cell to communicate with other parts of the cell.
The second type of chromatid, known as heterochrome, is more loosely packed and is more permissive to the transcription of messenger RNA.

Inside the nucleus of a cell, the DNA is wound around histone proteins.
This forms a structure called chromatin that allows the long DNA strands to fit inside the cell.
Variations in chromatin structure also help the cell to control the functional properties of DNA.
For example, a large proportion of chromatin in the cell is in the form of heterochromatin, which is very densely packed, and is associated with many roles such as gene silencing and keeping DNA intact during reproduction.
Many animals and plants have two copies of each DNA molecule: one inherited from the mother, and one from the father of the organism.
Reproductive cells undergo a process called recombination when they form, where the matching copies of each DNA molecule break in a number of places and rejoin to form a new ‘blend’ of their mother's and their father's DNA, which is passed on to their own offspring.
In contrast, most heterochromatin is inherited without recombining, preserving it in an unaltered form.
This is important since recombination in heterochromatin can create genetic abnormalities.
Adding small chemical modifications—such as methyl groups—to the histone proteins at the core of the chromatin can change how the DNA is packed.

This has led to concerns that they are harmful to the environment and may lead to the loss of species diversity.

Monoculture, where only one type of crop is grown to the exclusion of any other organism, is a pillar of modern agriculture.
Yet this narrow focus disregards how complex inter-species interactions can increase crop yield and biodiversity while decreasing the need for fertilizers or pesticides.
For example, many farmers across Asia introduce carps, crabs, turtles or other freshwater grazers into their rice paddies.

Cognitive neuroscience is one of the most promising areas of research in neuroscience, where researchers are trying to understand how the brain works.
To do so, they often use computational models that can be trained to simulate brain activity.
These models are called ‘generative models’, and they can be used to generate simulations of brain activity, which can then be used as a basis for making predictions about the brain’s behavior.
However, generative models are limited by the fact that they do not have a closed-form likelihood function, which is a mathematical formula that tells us how likely an observation is to be true.
Instead, the model must be ‘likelihood-free’ – that is, it must be able to predict the likelihood of the observed behavior.
This means that the model cannot be trained on a large number of simulated brain activity data, which makes it difficult to make predictions about how the model will perform in the real world.
To overcome this limitation, computational methods called Bayesian inference have been developed.
These methods use computer simulations to generate approximate likelihoods, which are then used to approximate the true likelihood of a given model.

Cognitive neuroscience studies the links between the physical brain and cognition.
Computational models that attempt to describe the relationships between the brain and specific behaviours quantitatively is becoming increasingly popular in this field.
This approach may help determine the causes of certain behaviours and make predictions about what behaviours will be triggered by specific changes in the brain.
Many of the computational models used in cognitive neuroscience are built based on experimental data.
A good model can predict the results of new experiments given a specific set of conditions with few parameters.
Candidate models are often called ‘generative’: models that can simulate data.
However, typically, cognitive neuroscience studies require going the other way around: they need to infer models and their parameters from experimental data.
Ideally, it should also be possible to properly assess the remaining uncertainty over the parameters after having access to the experimental data.
To facilitate this, the Bayesian approach to statistical analysis has become popular in cognitive neuroscience.
Common software tools for Bayesian estimation require a ‘likelihood function’, which measures how well a model fits experimental data for given values of the unknown parameters.

Ebola virus disease is caused by a group of viruses that infect the blood of humans and other animals, including bats, monkeys and other primates.
The disease is highly contagious and can lead to death in up to 90% of those infected.
The most recent outbreak of Ebola virus disease ( EVD ) began in Guinea in December 2013, and has since spread to Liberia, Sierra Leone and Nigeria.
This outbreak is the largest recorded in the history of the World Health Organization ( WHO ) and has been declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern by the WHO on 8 August 2014.

These viruses infect bats in the Democratic Republic of the Congo ( DRC ) and Sudan, and have been responsible for a number of outbreaks of the disease in humans in the 1970s and 1980s.
Previous studies have shown that bats can transmit the virus to humans from infected monkeys to humans.
