Vision is a highly complex, active process.
As we observe and interact with the world around us, we constantly use eye movements to capture the visual information we need.
In fact, our eyes continue to make tiny, unconscious movements even when we try to fix our gaze on something.
There are two main types of tiny eye movements.
The first kind, so called microsaccades, are fast, microscopic flicks that happen every second or half-second.
The other kind, termed drift, is a slower, gradual motion that takes place between microsaccades, or at any time when other eye movements are not happening.
However, we know far less about drifts than about any other eye movements: both the reason why they occur and the brain mechanisms controlling them are still unclear.
Many scientists think that drifts are largely random movements, without any set direction.

Cells are made up of two sets of chromosomes, each containing a different set of genes.

Insects communicate with each other using chemical signals called pheromones, which are released by female moths to attract the attention of male moths.
Female moths release a mixture of chemicals that attract males over a long distances, while male insects detect and respond to the chemical signals emitted by the female.
These signals are detected by olfactory receptors on the male antennae of the moth, which in turn detect the chemicals released by the females.
These receptors are called odorant receptors, and they are found in many different species of insects, including moths, beetles, bees and wasps.
However, it was not clear whether these receptors could also be found in male insects.
One type of odorant receptor, called the type I receptor, has been identified in the cotton leafworm Spodoptera littoralis.
This receptor is found on the tip of the male’s antennae and is tuned to the chemicals emitted by female cotton leafworms.
Previous studies have shown that this receptor is only found in males of this species.

– including some of the researchers involved in the previous work – have used a combination of genetic engineering and in vivo experiments to identify a new type I odor

Many animals make use of chemical signals to communicate with other members of their species.
Such chemical signals, called pheromones, often allow males and females of the same species to recognize each other before mating.
Since the discovery of the very first pheromone in the silkworm moth Bombyx mori at the end of the 1950s, moths have been a model for pheromone research.
The sex pheromone communication system in these insects has thus been well described: females emit a mixture of volatile chemicals, which can be detected by the antennae of males up to several hundred meters away.
This detection is achieved through neurons with specialized proteins known as pheromone receptors that bind to the chemical signals produced by the females.
Recognizing mates by detecting a very specific pheromone signature prevents moths from interbreeding with other species.
The evolution of pheromone signals and their corresponding receptors can therefore lead to the rise of new reproductive barriers between populations, and eventually to the emergence of new species.
The rate at which sex pheromones have diversified is likely one reason for the existence of over 160, 000 species of moths.

Cell membranes are made up of two layers of molecules called lipid bilayers.
The outer leaflet contains a protein called glycogen and the inner leaflet is made of two different molecules called phosphatidylcholine and sphingomyelin.
These two molecules are arranged in such a way that they bind to each other to form a structure called a helix, which allows them to move between the two leaflets.
In eukaryotic cells, this helix structure is asymmetrical, with the outermost leaflet being enriched in glycogen while the innermost is enriched in sphingomegalene.
This asymmetry in the distribution of phospholipids between the outer and inner leaflets of the membrane is essential for the cell to work properly.
To overcome this asymmetry, the cell uses a process called phosphoripid scrambling to move the two molecules of glycogen from one leaflet to the other.
This process is regulated by an enzyme called ANO6, which is encoded by a family of proteins called the ANO/TMEM16 family.
However, it is not clear whether this enzyme is a phosphorlipid scramblase or an ion channel.

Cell membranes are made of two layers of molecules called phospholipids.
The types of phospholipid molecules in the outer layer are often different from those in the inner layer.
This asymmetry is an important feature of most membranes, but cells also have proteins called ‘scramblases’ that can move ( or scramble ) the phospholipids between the two layers.
This scrambling process often marks a cell for destruction but also plays a key role in many activities throughout the body including cell–cell fusion, blood clotting, autoimmune diseases and inflammation.
Previous research revealed that a membrane protein called ANO6 is needed for some kinds of phospholipid scrambling.
Other proteins that are most closely related to ANO6 are not phospholipid scramblases; instead they are channel proteins that allow ions to pass across cell membranes.
ANO6 can also allow ions to flow across membranes, which raised the question: is ANO6 actually a scramblase itself, or does it control other proteins with scramblase activity?
Yu, Whitlock et al.
addressed this question by engineering human cells grown in the laboratory to produce the ANO6 protein, and found that these cells had high levels of phospholipid scrambling.

Branching morphogenesis is an important part of the development of many organs, including the kidneys, lungs and mammary glands.
This process begins with the formation of a bud-like structure known as the ureteric bud, which then proceeds to grow and divide, usually by bifurcation.
The resulting network of interconnected epithelial tubules serves to break up the larger tissue masses in which they form, facilitating the exchange of oxygen ( lungs ) and nutrients ( blood ) and the transport of waste ( kidneys ) or secretions ( glands ).
In some organs, such as the mammary gland, branching morphogenesis occurs as a self-organizing process.
In others, it is a random process that is driven by the interaction of cells at the tips of the bud and the surrounding tissue.
This suggests that branching may be a unifying feature of organs, but it is not clear whether this is the case in all organs.
To investigate this question, Hannezo et al.
examined the role of nephrons in kidney development in mice.
Nephrons are a group of specialized cells that form at the tip of the kidney, and form a network of tubules that connect the tip to the rest of the organ.

During development and before birth, many organs develop from branched tubes.
Whether forming the airways of the lungs, the collecting ducts of the kidneys or the milk ducts of the breast, there are many similarities between these structures.
Given their shared tree-like structures, one possibility is that these tissues all form through the same general process.
A key challenge is understanding why branched networks develop and pattern in such a way as to assume their functional roles in the adult organ.
A unifying theory, which proposes that certain tips stop growing in a random manner, has been proposed to solve this problem.
In this theory, the branched mammary gland structures stop growing when the tips of the structure impinge on neighbouring branches.
In the kidney, this cessation has been proposed to occur when nephrons – the structures that filter urine from blood – form near the end of the collecting ducts.
By growing kidneys in the laboratory and studying developing kidneys in mice, Short et al.
investigated whether nephrons do affect collecting duct growth and branch development.
The results of these experiments instead suggest that nephron formation has no effect on duct growth or branching.
The nephrons also do not appear to affect how quickly the duct cells grow and divide.

Vesicles are tiny compartments in the outer membrane of the cell that allow the cell’s internal organs to communicate with each other and with the outside world.
These compartments are surrounded by a network of compartments called the Golgi, which contains many different types of vesicles.
The Golgi is decorated with coiled-coil-like proteins that can extend long distances to help the vesicle find their targets.
One of these proteins, GCC185, is required for the transport of mannose 6-phosphate receptors ( MPRs ) from late endosomes to the trans Golgi network, where they can be used to detect and interact with other molecules in the cell.
However, it was not clear how GCC185 was able to do this, or how it could be so flexible that it could attach to the membrane of another cell.

used a technique called atomic force microscopy to examine the structure of GCC185.

Some cells release molecules, such as hormones and neurotransmitters, to signal to other cells and influence how they work.
As part of the release process, these molecules are packaged into small, balloon-like structures called vesicles.
Such vesicles move around within cells and are able to find the right place to release their contents to the outside.
A cellular compartment called the Golgi complex helps to prepare proteins for release from the cell.
Vesicles can bind to tethering proteins on the surface of the Golgi, but it was not clear how these proteins are able to capture the correct kind of vesicle.
The prediction was that the proteins are rigid, shaped like pipe cleaners that stick out from the Golgi as a meshwork that traps vesicles.

This revealed that this protein is not rod-like; it is instead rather floppy, and has two arms at one end that may ‘hug’ the incoming vesicle.

showed that this protein needs its middle portion to be floppy to work correctly.

The cellular Potts model is a computer simulation tool that allows scientists to study complex biological processes at the microscopic level.
It is a powerful tool for studying how cells grow and divide, and it has been used to study everything from the formation of slime moulds to the growth of blood vessels.
However, it is not easy to use because it requires a lot of programming skills, and because it relies on the C++ programming language.
To make it easier to use, many C++-based modelling frameworks exist that require the programmer to know how to code in C++, which makes them difficult to use in practice.
To overcome this limitation, some researchers have developed ‘explorable’ CPM models that allow users to change the parameters of the model in real time.
These models can then be shared via a web browser or saved locally, and can be viewed in real-time.
Now, Wortel et al.
have developed an open-source JavaScript library called ‘Artistoo’ that can be used to build interactive cellular potts models in the web browser.
These simulations can be published on any web server, saved locally and run on any device that supports web browsers.
They can also be shared with other CPM-based models
ref:
Understanding complex systems, such as the weather or the spread of a pandemic, often relies on computational models that can simulate what is happening and what will happen next.
Models like these can also be used to investigate biological processes.
For example, cellular Potts models ( or CPMs for short ) are regularly used to simulate how cells move, self-organise to form tissues and respond to their surroundings.
Computational biologists use a range of specialist skills and software to create these models.
However, this can make it difficult for people who do not have programming experience to interact with these simulations and incorporate them in to their own research.
If more people could engage with these models, this could help foster closer collaborations and ultimately lead to better models of biological systems.
To make CPMs more accessible, Wortel and Textor created a toolbox called Artistoo that allows users to view and interact with simulations using just an internet browser.
These simulations are very easy to interact with, and do not require any prior programming knowledge or specialised software.
Viewers can input different parameters in to the simulation and watch in real time to see how this affects the biological system being modelled.

Parkinson's disease is the second most common neurodegenerative illness in the world.
It is caused by the loss of nerve cells called dopamine neurons in the brainstem, which are responsible for the release of the chemical messenger messenger molecule dopamine.
However, Parkinson’s disease also affects several other brain regions, including the midbrain, the basal ganglia, and the cortex.
Previous studies have shown that these brain regions show signs of atrophy in the early stages of the disease, but it is not clear how the disease spreads from these areas to other parts of the brain.
One possibility is that the disease may spread from the middle brainstem to the rest of the cerebral cortex, which is thought to be where the disease originates.
This is supported by the network-spread hypothesis, which proposes that the brain is organized into networks of cells that communicate with each other, and that these networks are vulnerable to the spread of Parkinson's disease.
To test this hypothesis, Goedert et al.
compared the brains of 232 newly diagnosed patients with and 117 healthy volunteers from the Parkinson's Progression Markers Initiative ( PPMI ), a national registry of people with and without the disease.

Although Parkinson's disease is the second most common neurodegenerative disorder, its cause is not known and there is no cure.
The symptoms of Parkinson's disease, which include tremor and slowing of voluntary movements, get progressively worse over time.
The numbers of neurons in certain brain regions also decrease, causing those parts of the brain to shrink; this is known as ‘atrophy’.
However, no conclusive signs of atrophy have been found in the brains of people in the early stages of the disease.
One theory suggests that Parkinson's disease is caused by a toxic protein that is able to spread from neuron to neuron.
Recent advances in brain imaging have made it possible to map networks in the living human brain—the so-called brain connectome.
These networks could form the ‘highways’ through which a disease-causing agent might spread.
The Parkinson's Progression Markers Initiative ( PPMI ) is a large study that collects data from hundreds of people in an effort to identify the causes of Parkinson's disease.
Zeighami et al.
have now analyzed MRI scans that were collected as part of this initiative, which show the structure of the brains of 230 people in the early stages of Parkinson's disease.

Bacteria need to adapt to changes in their environment to survive.
One way they do this is by changing the way their genes are switched on and off.
This is done by a protein called histone-like Nucleoid Structuring Protein ( H-NS ), which is found in bacteria called Escherichia coli, Salmonella and other Gram-negative bacteria.
The H-Nucleoid Structure protein binds to the DNA in long sections of the bacterial genome, which allows it to form loops that allow it to bind to other DNA molecules.
This allows the bacteria to divide and multiply.

The genetic information every cell needs to work properly is encoded in molecules of DNA that are much longer than the cell itself.
A key challenge in biology is to understand how DNA is organized to fit inside each cell, whilst still providing access to the information that it contains.
Since the way DNA is organized can influence which genes are active, rearranging DNA plays an important role in controlling how cells behave.
In Escherichia coli and many other bacteria, a protein called H-NS contributes to DNA reorganization by forming or rupturing loops in the DNA in response to changes in temperature, the levels of salt and other aspects of the cell’s surroundings.

The kidneys of humans and other mammals are made up of cells called progenitor cells, which start out as stem cells and then develop into specialized cells known as nephrons.
These progenitors form a cap around the tip of the ureter, which is surrounded by cells called cap mesenchymal cells.
These cells then undergo a process known as epithelialization to become epithelialized cells that form the lining of the kidney.
The epithelialised cells then divide to form new cells called nephron cells.
This process is controlled by a signalling molecule called WNT9b that is released from the end of the Ureter.
This molecule activates a signalling pathway called β-catenin, which in turn activates a signaling molecule called RSPO1, which activates the WNT/β-Catenin signalling pathway.
This pathway is essential for the formation of the neephron cells, but it is not clear exactly how it works.

Kidneys filter waste out of the bloodstream to produce urine.
Each kidney contains many structures called nephrons which separate the waste from the blood.
The number of nephrons in a kidney varies between people, and those with low numbers have a higher risk of chronic kidney disease.
Nephrons are formed before birth from a specific group of so-called progenitor cells.
Each of these cells can either divide to make others like itself, or it can specialize to make nephron cells.
At the end of embryonic kidney development, all the progenitor cells become nephron cells.
Cells that specialize to become part of a nephron first go through a change called a mesenchyme-to-epithelial transition.
Epithelial cells move less than mesenchymal cells, and also develop a clear structure where the two ends of the cell adapt to different roles.
Evidence suggests that a cell communication process called WNT/β-catenin signaling controls this transition.
Yet the details of how this transition is controlled are not fully understood.

Vision depends on the ability of the retina, the layer of cells that line the back of the eye, to detect light and detect changes in the surrounding environment.
The retina is made up of two layers: the photoreceptor layer, which contains the cells that detect light, and the choroid, which receives oxygen and nutrients from the blood.
The photoreceptors are surrounded by a thin layer of epithelium, which protects them from damage and allows them to receive signals from the outside world.
However, some of the blood vessels in the inner retina extend into the retinal layer, and this can lead to the formation of abnormal blood vessels that can damage the retina and cause vision loss.
A protein called vascular endothelial growth factor A ( or VEGF-A for short ) plays a crucial role in the growth of blood vessels within the retina.
In healthy eyes, this protein is released from the cells of the choriocapillaris, which is the largest blood vessel in the eye.
In the eyes of people with age-related macular degeneration ( AMD ), however, it is not clear how it is produced and how it affects vision.

The inner surface of the vertebrate eye is lined with a multilayered structure known as the retina.
The bottom layer of the retina is composed of rods and cones—neurons that are directly sensitive to light—and is called the photoreceptor layer.
Rods function primarily in dim light and provide black-and-white vision, while cones support daytime vision and are responsible for colour perception.
Unlike the upper layers of the retina, the photoreceptor layer does not contain blood vessels: oxygen and nutrients are instead provided by a structure just underneath the retina called the choroid.
The eye relies on the rods and cones converting light into electrical signals, and the photoreceptor layer must remain free of blood vessels for this process to work properly.
If blood vessels extend into the photoreceptor layer from rest of the retina ( which is above it ) or the choroid ( below ), they can disrupt the retina and give rise to a condition called age-related macular degeneration, which is a leading cause of irreversible blindness in adults.

Cells in plants, animals and other eukaryotes, such as fungi and algae, can grow to a variety of sizes, but it is not clear how they do so.
In fungi, for example, the size of the daughter cells is controlled by a protein called Whi5p, which prevents them from dividing.
In budding yeast, however, it was thought that this protein was responsible for limiting the number of daughter cells that could divide.
Now, Schmoller et al.
have identified a new protein called CDKG1, which helps yeast cells to control how many daughters they produce.
This protein is found in the nucleus of yeast cells, and it is thought to regulate how many daughter cells a yeast cell can produce by controlling the concentration of a protein that is found on the surface of the mother cell.
In yeast, this protein is known as retinoblastoma-1, or RB for short.
When RB is missing, the yeast cells produce fewer daughters and fewer mitotic cells, which means that they are less likely to divide.
However, when RB is present, the daughter yeast cells are able to divide more often and produce more daughters.
This suggests that RB is responsible for controlling the number and size of daughters.

Most cells are programmed to maintain a certain size.
This property, known as size control, is achieved by balancing growth and division, such that a cell will only divide after it reaches a certain size.
However, and despite years of research, it is largely unknown how cells sense their size ( or growth ) to be able to divide accordingly.
One theory proposes that there is a “sizer” protein inside cells, and that cells measure the abundance of this protein and use it to link cell size to the process of division.

Cells called human pluripotent stem cells ( hPSCs ) are a type of stem cell that can develop into any type of cell in the body.
These cells can be grown in a variety of different conditions, including in a dish or in the laboratory, and can be used to make any number of different types of tissues, including bone, muscle, cartilage, and cartilage.
However, it is not clear how well these stem cells can recreate the process of vertebral vertebra formation in the human embryo.
One way to study this process is to grow human embryonic stem cells in a culture medium that mimics the development of the vertebral column in the embryo.
To do this, the cells are first grown in two-dimensional ( 2D ) cultures, and then grown in three-dimensional cultures ( 3D ).
This allows researchers to study how the vertebrae form and how they develop in the developing embryo.

Humans are part of a group of animals called vertebrates, which are all the animals with backbones.
Broadly, all vertebrates have a similar body shape with a head at one end and a left and right side that are similar to each other.
Although this is not very obvious in humans, vertebrate bodies are derived from pairs of segments arranged from the head to the tail.
Each of these segments or somites originates early in embryonic development.
Cells from each somite then divide, grow and specialize to form bones such as the vertebrae of the vertebral column, muscles, skin, and other tissues that make up each segment.
Studying different animals during embryonic development has provided insights into how somites form and grow, but it is technically difficult to do and only provides an approximate model of how somites develop in humans.
Being able to make and study somites using human cells in the lab would help scientists learn more about how somite formation in humans is regulated.

Brain-computer interfaces ( or BCIs for short ) are a type of communication device that can help people with paralysis to communicate with others.
BCIs can be used to restore communication between the brain and the rest of the body by translating the activity of the brain into signals that can be interpreted by computers.
Previous studies have shown promising results, but it is not clear whether the technology is ready for widespread adoption by people with physical limitations of speech.
Here, Nuyujukian et al.
developed a BCI that could be used by three people with a paralysis of the anarthria, a condition that affects the ability to move the body’s muscles.
The BCI was able to control the movements of the participants’ hands, and they were able to type and type rapidly and accurately.
The results of the experiments show that the BCI could be useful for people with limited speech, and that it could potentially be used as an assistive device for people who are unable to speak.
The system could also be used for other tasks, such as writing and typing, and could be adapted for use with a computer.
The technology is still in its early stages of development, and it is unclear whether it is ready to be used in humans
ref:
People with various forms paralysis not only have difficulties getting around, but also are less able to use many communication technologies including computers.
In particular, strokes, neurological injuries, or diseases such as ALS can lead to severe paralysis and make it very difficult to communicate.
In rare instances, these disorders can result in a condition called locked-in syndrome, in which the affected person is aware but completely unable to move or speak.
Several researchers are looking to help people with severe paralysis to communicate again, via a system called a brain-computer interface.
These devices record activity in the brain either from the surface of the scalp or directly using a sensor that is surgically implanted.
Computers then interpret this activity via algorithms to generate signals that can control various tools, including robotic limbs, powered wheelchairs or computer cursors.
Such tools would be invaluable for many people with paralysis.

set out to study the performance of an implanted brain-computer interface in three people with varying forms of paralysis and focused specifically on a typing task.

In many animals, hunting is an innate behaviour that is crucial for survival.
This involves a sequence of specialised oculomotor and locomotor actions, which culminate in a series of discrete orienting turns and approach swims, followed by a kinematically distinct capture swim.
The brain circuits that control this behaviour are poorly understood, but have been identified in a variety of animal species, ranging from courtship songs of fruit flies to parental behaviours of mice.
In zebrafish, for example, the larvae of the Zebrafish family of fish start hunting by rapidly converging their eyes, which substantially increases their binocular visual field.
A high vergence angle is maintained during this process, which allows the larvae to focus their eyes on a specific feature of the prey.
The larvae then turn and swim towards the target, which is then captured by the fish’s tail.

Hunting is an innate behaviour that relies on predators executing a precise set of actions to identify, approach, target, and strike prey.
In vertebrates ( animals with a backbone ), the identity of the brain circuits that trigger hunting is still unclear.
These networks are thought to link sensory perception ( seeing prey ) with a specialised action ( starting an attack ).
Larval zebrafish are a good model in which to study these circuits, because they have a tiny, transparent brain where neurons can be observed in real time.

harnessed a technique called optogenetics to artificially turn on these brain cells in the pretectum, which caused the fish to start hunting even when prey was absent.

Drug screening is the process by which scientists identify promising new drugs that have the potential to improve the lives of humans and other animals.
These drugs are often tested under a wide range of conditions, such as the presence of different chemicals or the addition of small molecules or inhibitory RNAs.
However, it can be difficult to determine the effects of a given drug on a given biological target, since it is not known how the effects on one target will generalize to other targets.
One way to overcome this problem is to use a technique called active learning, in which a computer program learns how to perform experiments on a large number of different targets and conditions.
This approach can be used to identify new drug targets, but it is still not clear how it will work.
To address this problem, Chen et al.
developed an algorithm that can learn to perform small sets of experiments on different targets, and then use these experiments to learn how the drug affects the target.

Biomedical scientists have invested significant effort into making it easy to perform lots of experiments quickly and cheaply.
These “high throughput” methods are the workhorses of modern “systems biology” efforts.
However, we simply cannot perform an experiment for every possible combination of different cell type, genetic mutation and other conditions.
In practice this has led researchers to either exhaustively test a few conditions or targets, or to try to pick the experiments that best allow a particular problem to be explored.
But which experiments should we pick?
The ones we think we can predict the outcome of accurately, the ones for which we are uncertain what the results will be, or a combination of the two?
Humans are not particularly well suited for this task because it requires reasoning about many possible outcomes at the same time.
However, computers are much better at handling statistics for many experiments, and machine learning algorithms allow computers to “learn” how to make predictions and decisions based on the data they’ve previously processed.

When an animal experiences an unpleasant smell, it is important for it to be able to associate it with food or a reward.

When a sperm cell and an egg cell unite, each contributes half of the genetic material needed for the fertilised egg to develop.
This creates opportunities for new and beneficial genetic combinations to arise.
To ensure that each new sperm or egg has half a set of chromosomes, reproductive cells undergo a special type of division called meiosis.

Second, they help to hold the chromosomes together at a key point of meiosis, reducing the chances that the wrong number of chromosomes ends up in a sperm or egg cell.

Hybridization is the process by which two closely related species or groups of organisms can produce offspring that are genetically distinct from each other.
In plants, animals and fungi, this process is called hybridization, and it can lead to changes in the genetic material of the offspring.
For example, hybridization can introduce mutations into the genomes of the parents of the two offspring.

Hybrids arise when two populations of organisms that are related but genetically different mate and produce offspring.

In fact, the inability of two organisms to mate and produce viable and fertile hybrids has been used as a way to define species.
